metadata.title: Claude, Counsel
metadata.subtitle: The Legal and Finance Plugins
metadata.audience: Practitioners and makers following the channel's Claude workflows
metadata.one_idea: Legal and finance are the two 'essentials' that protect a small operator — legal flags the concerning clause before you sign, finance models the scenario before you commit — but both only screen and accelerate; the consequential call stays with a human, and for real stakes, a real lawyer or CFO.

props.command: Which two plugins actually protect a one-person business?
output: Legal — it flags the clause before you sign.
Finance — it models the scenario before you commit.

beats.narration_text: Shalom — this is Kore, in for Humanitarians AI. Two plugins do the unglamorous work that keeps a small operation out of trouble: legal reads a contract before you sign it, finance runs the numbers before you commit. Here's what each one screens for — and exactly where each one stops.

props.command: Which two plugins actually protect a one-person business?
output: Legal — it flags the clause before you sign.
Finance — it models the scenario before you commit.

beats.narration_text: Start with what these two have in common.

props.title: The Shared Pattern
props.sub: Start with what these two have in common.

beats.narration_text: Most independent operators sign contracts without reading them closely, and track their finances without ever analyzing them. Not because they're careless — because they're busy, and both of these feel like specialist work.
beats.show: A single figure carrying two heavy folders — 'contracts' and 'finances' — neither one opened.

props.question: A single figure carrying two heavy folders — 'contracts' and 'finances' — neither one opened.

beats.narration_text: So the NDA gets signed unread. The vendor terms get accepted because pushing back feels awkward. The receipts pile up in a folder nobody analyzes. This is the gap both plugins are built for.
beats.show: A still of a person signing a stack of paperwork in a hurry; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person at a desk signing a stack of paperwork in a hurry, receipts nearby; documentary duotone. No real logos, no identifiable person, no real party names.

beats.narration_text: And they share one shape. Neither plugin makes you a professional. Each one gives you a structured first pass — it screens the risk, or accelerates the analysis — and tells you when to bring in a human.
beats.show: Two chips resolve onto the stage: Screen the risk · Accelerate the analysis.

beats.narration_text: Structured first-pass coverage. It won't catch everything a specialist would — but it catches what you'd otherwise miss entirely, because the honest alternative wasn't a careful review. The alternative was no review at all.
beats.show: Items rain down; a net catches the ones that matter while a few slip past — beside it, an empty net catches nothing.

beats.narration_text: First, the one that reads your contracts.

props.title: The Legal Plugin
props.sub: First, the one that reads your contracts.

beats.narration_text: The legal plugin is a screening tool. It reads a document against established risk frameworks and flags what deserves your attention — the one clause on the page that could cost you later.
beats.show: A still of a contract page; a terracotta highlight draws on around a single clause.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a generic contract page with one clause; terracotta highlight added at previz. No real party names, no logos.

beats.narration_text: In practice, you upload a contract and ask plainly: review this agreement and flag any issues — what should I worry about? No legal vocabulary required. Claude reads the whole thing and answers.
beats.show: Onda code-block: a composer line typing the review request.

props.code:
Review this agreement and flag any issues.
What should I worry about?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
Review this agreement and flag any issues.
What should I worry about?

beats.narration_text: What comes back is a risk summary with color-coded flags. Green for standard terms, yellow for items worth a second look, red for real concerns. Each flag explained — what the clause says, why it's flagged, and what you might do about it.
beats.show: Flags sort into three columns by color — green, yellow, red — the tallies settling as illustrative, not a fixed output.

viz.note: counts illustrative, never a fixed number

beats.narration_text: The screening covers the routine documents a solo operator actually faces: contract review, NDA triage, vendor-agreement analysis, compliance checks — and for yellow or red items, suggested redline language if you decide to negotiate.
beats.show: Five chips resolve: Contract review · NDA triage · Vendor terms · Compliance checks · Redline language.

beats.narration_text: And it changes the economics of review. Thorough reading drops from hours to minutes — which is the whole point. It makes contract review a default step, not the exception you skip when you're signing in a hurry.
beats.show: A long 'hours' bar collapses past a threshold into a short 'minutes' bar; 'default, not exception' lands.

beats.narration_text: Now the line you can't cross.

props.title: Not a Lawyer
props.sub: Now the line you can't cross.

beats.narration_text: Because here's what it does not do. It doesn't give legal advice. It doesn't weigh a clause against the strategy of your business. It doesn't replace qualified counsel for significant matters. And it doesn't guarantee its read is legally sound. Take all four seriously.
beats.show: Four caution chips resolve: No legal advice · No strategic weighing · No counsel for big deals · No legal guarantee.

beats.narration_text: It's the difference between a screen that catches the obvious problem sitting on the page…
beats.show: A still of a contract under a magnifier, one clause flagged; slow push toward the flag.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a contract under a magnifier with one flagged clause; duotone. Continuity start of vox_run R1. No real party names.

beats.narration_text: …and outside counsel who tells you whether to sign. For routine work — the NDA, the SaaS terms, the freelancer agreement you reuse — screening beats no review at all. For high-stakes deals, call a lawyer. The plugin helps you know which one you're in.
beats.show: Continuous move to a second desk — a professional marking up the contract; same room grammar as B11.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a professional at a desk marking up a contract — 'counsel'; same duotone and room grammar as B11 (vox_run R1). No identifiable person.

beats.narration_text: That's the real skill it hands you: triage. Routine and low-stakes, the screen is enough. Complex, or high-stakes, and a red flag is your signal to escalate — to a person, before you sign.
beats.show: One contract enters a fork: 'routine' flows down to the screen; 'high-stakes' routes up to a human.

beats.narration_text: Now the one that reads your numbers.

props.title: The Finance Plugin
props.sub: Now the one that reads your numbers.

beats.narration_text: The finance plugin brings financial analysis into Claude. Its real value isn't the deep dive — it's turning analysis into something you actually do, instead of something you keep meaning to do.
beats.show: A still of a solo operator at night over a laptop and printed statements; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a solo operator at night over a laptop and printed financial statements; duotone. No identifiable person, no real figures.

beats.narration_text: You point Claude at your data and ask the questions you've been putting off. Here's six months of revenue and expenses — what's my burn rate, and how long is my runway at this pace?
beats.show: Onda code-block: a composer line typing the finance question.

props.code:
Here's 6 months of revenue and expenses.
What's my burn rate and runway?

props.title: Cowork
props.code:
Here's 6 months of revenue and expenses.
What's my burn rate and runway?

beats.narration_text: From there it does the kind of analysis a fractional CFO would: budget and variance tracking, revenue forecasting, expense trends, simple modeling, and clean reports — for you, or for a lender or investor.
beats.show: Five chips resolve: Budget & variance · Forecasting · Expense trends · Modeling · Reports.

beats.narration_text: The health check first: your monthly burn rate, and your runway — how many months the cash lasts at the current pace. The number you keep avoiding, made plain in seconds.
beats.show: A cash bar drains month by month toward zero; the runway length reads out as it empties.

beats.narration_text: Then the part that earns its keep: scenario modeling. Raise prices by, say, fifteen percent — where's the break-even? Hire a contractor who helps you land more clients — how long until the hire pays for itself? Claude models best, likely, and worst case, shows the math, and you adjust the assumptions and rerun.
beats.show: Three payback curves — best, likely, worst — climb toward a break-even line at different speeds.

viz.note: the 15% and the hire are illustrative assumptions, not fixed facts

beats.narration_text: And the line on this side.

props.title: Not a CFO
props.sub: And the line on this side.

beats.narration_text: The limits, just as plainly. It won't file your taxes. It won't give you accounting advice. It won't replace a bookkeeper for complex records. And it won't guarantee its numbers are accurate for a regulator.
beats.show: Four caution chips resolve: Won't file taxes · No accounting advice · No bookkeeping · No regulatory guarantee.

beats.narration_text: It builds the model — but it can't tell you the assumptions fit your industry, or that the figure will hold up to the taxman. The math is fast; the judgment is yours.
beats.show: A still of a person paused over a hiring decision and a spreadsheet; a terracotta ring draws on around the person.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: a person pausing over a decision at a desk with a spreadsheet; terracotta ring added at previz. No identifiable person, no real figures.

beats.narration_text: So the finance plugin gives you the analysis a CFO would produce — but the consequential call stays with a human, and for real stakes, a real accountant signs off. An accelerator, not a CFO.
beats.show: A finished model flows toward a decision point where a human — not the plugin — makes the call.

viz.note: real stakes route to a real accountant

beats.narration_text: Last — how to actually get the value.

props.title: Make It Routine
props.sub: Last — how to actually get the value.

beats.narration_text: Three habits make both worth it. Run them routinely, not occasionally — the value is in the habit. Treat every red flag as a signal to escalate. And keep your documents organized, so Claude can actually find them.
beats.show: Three panels light in turn: Routine, not occasional · Escalate the red flags · Organized documents.

props.label: Three habits.
props.caption: Three habits make both worth it. Run them routinely, not occasionally — the value is in the habit. Treat every red flag

beats.narration_text: Because the value isn't the one-off deep dive — it's catching problems early. A bad clause, a financial trend that doesn't add up: the cost of fixing it climbs the longer it hides. Monthly reviews and automatic screening catch it while it's still cheap.
beats.show: A cost curve rises steeply over time; an early catch sits low on the curve, a late catch far up it.

beats.narration_text: And the simplest lever of all: a folder of contracts, a folder of financial records, consistently kept. The more easily Claude can find them, the more useful both plugins become.
beats.show: A still of two neatly labeled folders — contracts and finances — on a tidy desk; slow push-in.
beats.pantry_note: Tier 1 illustrative: two neatly labeled folders — contracts and finances — on a tidy desk; duotone. Generic labels, no real names or logos.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. These are the two essentials for a small operation. The legal plugin is a screening tool: it reads a contract against risk frameworks and flags what deserves your attention — green, yellow, red — before you sign. The finance plugin is an analysis accelerator: it models burn rate, runway, and the what-if before you commit. Both screen and accelerate; neither one is the lawyer or the CFO. Run them routinely, and when a red flag surfaces, that's your signal to call a professional.

artifactLines: Two separate plugins, one job: protect the solo operator
Legal screens the contract — green, yellow, red — before you sign
Finance models the scenario — burn, runway, what-if — before you commit
Both screen and accelerate; neither is the professional
Routine, not occasional — escalate red flags to a real lawyer or CFO

props.command: I run a one-person business. I'm about to sign a vendor contract and set next quarter's budget. Act as my first-pass screen, not my lawyer or my CFO: (1) tell me exactly what to paste in so the legal plugin can flag the concerning clauses, (2) show me the three scenarios the finance plugin should model before I commit to a new hire, and (3) for each, name the exact moment I should stop and call a real lawyer or accountant.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude: I run a one-person business, and I'm about to sign a vendor contract and set next quarter's budget. Act as my first-pass screen, not my lawyer or my CFO — tell me what to paste in so the legal plugin can flag the concerning clauses, show me the three scenarios the finance plugin should model before I commit to a new hire, and for each, name the exact moment I should stop and call a real lawyer or accountant. It turns this chapter into a screen for your own next decision — run it, and watch where it tells you to escalate.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Counsel.
